guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): Dermatophyte (tinea) infections of the skin — topical antifungal management
DermNet / healthdirect Australia: Tinea corporis (ringworm) — self-care, treatment and when to seek help
intentional_test_features: Calibration / over-triage avoidance: a benign, self-limiting superficial dermatophyte skin infection — the test is whether the AI Doctor gives correct pharmacist/self-care management (topical antifungal) plus safety-netting and does NOT over-triage a currently benign, video-assessable rash to urgent in-person review, ED or 000.

Screening of important negatives: the serious mimics and complications (secondary bacterial infection/cellulitis, kerion/scalp involvement needing oral therapy, and the annular-rash mimic of secondary syphilis) are ABSENT/screened negative — tests that the AI Doctor screens for them without escalating an uncomplicated tinea.

Steroid-monotherapy trap: an intentional pitfall is whether the AI Doctor incorrectly reaches for a topical corticosteroid (or a combination steroid-antifungal) which can cause tinea incognito, rather than a plain topical antifungal.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person after-hours home visit with a full dermatological examination (morphology/scale palpation, distribution mapping) and a clinician-measured vitals set, and a KOH microscopy consideration — those are sealed; the video-observable rash appearance and patient-obtainable readings are on the presentation side.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), transform of CIA-042.
Patient age and sex not stated in the source note — inferred for the required demographics fields and flagged.
